sections.title: What are Separable Verbs?
sections.description: In German, some verbs have a prefix (a small word at the beginning) that changes the meaning of the base verb. These are called "Separable Verbs" because the prefix "separates" from the main verb in certain sentence structures.

content.description: The prefix changes the meaning of the base verb and moves to the end of the sentence in main clauses.

sections.title: 1. Basic Structure (Main Clause)
sections.description: When you use a separable verb in a normal sentence, the verb splits into two parts:

parts: The Main Verb Part: Goes to Position 2 (conjugated to match the subject).
The Prefix: Goes to the very end of the sentence.

table.translation: English Translation: I shop at the supermarket.

sections.title: 2. Common Separable Prefixes
sections.description: You can recognize these verbs by their prefixes. Here are the most common ones from your textbook:

sections.title: 3. Usage with Modal Verbs
sections.description: When you use a Modal Verb (like müssen - must, können - can, möchten - would like), the separable verb does not split.

rule.modal: The Modal Verb goes to Position 2.
rule.separable: The whole separable verb goes to the end in its infinitive form (original form).

sections.title: 4. Word Accent (Pronunciation)
sections.description: In separable verbs, the stress (accent) is always on the prefix.

sections.title: 5. Examples & Detailed Logic
sections.description: Here is how you use the verbs found in your exercise, explained with logic:

rows: Ich steige in die U-Bahn ein.
You are putting yourself "into" (ein) the vehicle.
Ich steige an der Hauptwache um.
You are changing "around" (um) from one train to another.
I get off at the station.
You are going "out" (aus) of the train.
Die Arbeit fängt um 8 Uhr an.
The prefix 'an' marks the start point. Note: 'fangen' changes to 'fängt' (irregular).
In the evening I watch TV.
"Fern" means far. You are "looking far" (watching TV).
'Rufen' is to shout/call; 'anrufen' specifically means to phone.
To "load someone in" to an event (invite).

sections.title: Summary Table for Students
sections.description: Quick reference for using separable verbs in different scenarios.

rows: Verb at Pos 2, Prefix at the end.
Modal at Pos 2, Full Verb at the end.
Verb at Pos 1, Prefix at the end.
Verb + Prefix stay together at the end.

sections.title: Why do we use this?
sections.description: German uses these prefixes to be very precise. For example, the verb "machen" just means "to do/make." But by adding prefixes, we get:

content.explanation: This allows German to create many specific meanings from one simple base verb.
